summary:
The emergence of the first true cities in Mesopotamia during the fourth millennium BCE (4000-3000 BCE) represents a watershed in human social evolution — the appearance for the first time of permanent settlements large enough, specialized enough, and institutionally complex enough to qualify as urban rather than merely village or town communities. This urbanization process, centered on the alluvial plains of southern Mesopotamia, created a new mode of human existence that would spread outward to become the dominant form of complex societies across much of the world.

The distinguishing characteristics of these first cities went beyond mere size, though size was certainly a factor: [[event:1618|Uruk]] may have reached 40,000-80,000 inhabitants at its height. More fundamentally, cities differed from earlier large villages in their degree of economic specialization (full-time craftspeople, administrators, priests, and merchants who did not practice agriculture), their institutional complexity (temples, administrative offices, and redistributive systems that managed flows of goods across thousands of households), and their dependence on surrounding agricultural hinterlands for food.

The temple institution was the organizational nucleus of the earliest Mesopotamian cities. The great temple precincts of [[event:1617|Uruk]] served simultaneously as the city's primary economic managers, ritual centers, and administrative hubs, collecting agricultural surplus from dependent villages and villages within their hinterlands and redistributing it to the urban workforce of craftspeople and laborers. This redistributive economy, tracked through the [[event:1619|invention of writing]], was what made large-scale non-agricultural urban populations economically viable.

The first cities gave rise to new social formations: professional scribal classes, specialist craftspeople, long-distance merchants, and military organizations distinct from the general farming population. They also generated new forms of inequality — the sharp stratification between temple administrators and the laborers who received rations is visible in the very first written documents. Yet they also created the conditions for unprecedented levels of innovation, cultural exchange, and intellectual development, as dense populations of specialized individuals interacted and accumulated knowledge across generations. The city has remained humanity's primary engine of complexity ever since.
